I am grateful that, in 2026, some of the people who wrote and defended our Constitution are still here, still talking to us, still fighting for the same principles they risked their lives and careers for decades ago. 

I'm grateful—but I also feel a little guilty that after everything they've already given this country, we keep asking them for more.

On any given news day, I can turn on the TV or open a news site and see familiar names at the center of our biggest constitutional fights. 

Impeachment, political dynasties, Charter change, the West Philippine Sea, judicial overreach—again and again, we drag the elders back into the arena. 

Mahirap. Nakakapagod. Pero andiyan pa rin sila.

When a framer or a retired justice speaks, I don't hear just another pundit. I hear someone who argued over the text of the Charter, who remembers the fear and hope of 1986, and who has watched almost four decades of promises kept and broken. 

As a citizen and as a blogger, I feel like I'm freeloading on their moral capital more than I should.

This writing is not a hall of fame or a definitive roll call. These are simply some of the elders whose names keep appearing in my research and my feed, and leaving someone out is not a judgment on their contribution.

🟥 JUSTICE ADOLF AZCUNA AND OUR CONFUSED REPUBLIC

Recently, I watched Justice Adolfo Azcuna explain, calmly and patiently, how the Supreme Court's latest ruling on impeachment works. 

He walked viewers through the idea that the Court had effectively added “two additional ways” to initiate impeachment and clarified that the old “express lane” understanding from Francisco v. House wasn't simply erased, but reinterpreted in light of the Constitution.

This is a man who helped draft the 1987 Constitution and later served on the Supreme Court. 

Now, in 2026, he is still the one we turn to when the Court's own decision generates confusion instead of clarity. 

In the same breath, he reminds everyone that the House cannot pursue Charter change alone, that serious “Cha-cha” requires the Senate as a co-equal partner, and that any anti-dynasty law must regulate power without trampling on the people’s basic right to choose. 

I’m worried that we live in a country where the engineer who helped design the system is still needed to explain how the valves and switches work, because the current operators either don’t understand them or pretend not to.

🟥 ATTY. CHRISTIAN MONSOD AND THE UNFINISHED FIGHT AGAINST DYNASTIES

Then there is Christian Monsod, one of the framers and a former Comelec chair, who could have retired into occasional lectures and polite conferences. 

Instead, in February 2026, he went to the Senate and told our lawmakers to pass a law that completely abolishes political dynasties. 

He linked dynasties to our persistent poverty, inequality, and democratic decay, and he reminded everyone that the anti-dynasty mandate in the Constitution has been ignored for nearly four decades.

What I remember most is his admission in earlier interviews that the framers “trusted Congress too much” to define and ban dynasties. A safeguard they built into the Charter became a dead letter because the very people it restrains have to pass the implementing law. 

Instead of quietly living with that regret, Monsod keeps coming back, testifying, writing, and insisting that it’s not too late to correct the mistake. 

As a writer, I can quote constitutional provisions and talk about dynasties all day, but it’s different when the person who wrote that provision is standing in the Senate almost four decades later, saying: we misjudged you, and yet we still want you to do what’s right.

🟥 ATTY. RENE SARMIENTO AND THE CLOSING SAFETY VALVES

Another name that has been hard to ignore lately is Rene Sarmiento, also a framer and former Comelec commissioner. 

After the recent Supreme Court ruling on impeachment, he has been one of the voices warning that the decision risks making accountability “almost impossible” and may constitute judicial overreach. 

He has explained how the ruling complicates impeachment in a way that undercuts its role as a check on top officials.

When someone who helped design the Constitution's accountability mechanisms tells me that the safety valves are being sealed off, I don't feel like a detached observer. 

I feel like a homeowner watching the original engineer shaking his head at what the new contractors have done to the building. 

My usual lines about “checks and balances” start to feel less like theory and more like a structural emergency.

🟥 JUSTICE ANTONIO CARPIO AND THE NEVER-ENDING LESSON

For years now, Justice Antonio Carpio has been a constant presence in my reading on the West Philippine Sea and constitutional limits on power. 

Even in retirement, he has continued to lecture, write, and give interviews about everything from our maritime rights to the proper way to amend the Constitution. 

Recently, he has been pushing back against attempts to use people's initiative as a shortcut for Charter change, urging the Commission on Elections to dismiss defective petitions and warning against using the Constitution as a playground for narrow political interests.

He has also made it clear that while he is open to a carefully worded economic amendments, these should come with strict constitutional guardrails and honest public debate, not backroom deals. 

A few pieces I've written about the West Philippine Sea and charter change would be much weaker without Carpio's work. 

I keep going back to his arguments, his public statements, his lectures, and whenever he appears on screen again, I’m reminded that we have been relying on him for far longer than we had any right to expect.

🟥 THE QUIETER BUT STEADY CIRCLE

Around these more prominent names is a quieter circle of elders who still shape the way I think about democracy and governance. 

Former Chief Justice Hilario G. Davide Jr., another framer, continues to join intergenerational and international conversations on peace, the environment, and constitutional democracy. 

Dr. Florangel Rosario-Braid stays active in media and education, writing and speaking about freedom of expression, media ethics, and resisting authoritarian drift. 

Professor Edmundo “Ed” Garcia carries on with civic and peace education, trying to keep young people interested in rights and democracy. 

Economist Dr. Bernardo Villegas still links the constitutional framework to long-term economic policy and investment debates.

They may not trend on social media, but they shape the intellectual climate that people like me rely on whenever we sit down to write about good governance.

🟥 WHAT WE OWE THEM WHILE THEY ARE STILL HERE

It would be easy to end this with a simple “thank you” and a short list of names. But if all we offer these elders is a paragraph of praise while ignoring the warnings they keep repeating, that gratitude feels hollow. 

The fact that we still need people in their 70s and 80s to decode impeachment rules, explain Charter change, and plead for an anti-dynasty law says something uncomfortable about our institutions. 

It says we have not built enough younger voices in Congress, in the bar, in the bench, and in civil society who can speak with the same clarity and courage—and be heard.

Honoring them, I think, has to mean at least three things. 

First, we need to take their warnings seriously. 

When Monsod says political dynasties are at the heart of our persistent problems and calls for a law that truly dismantles them, we cannot treat that as just another quote for tomorrow’s news cycle. 

When Sarmiento and Azcuna point out how recent rulings can weaken accountability or distort impeachment, we can’t shrug it off as legalese. 

When Carpio and Davide remind us that Charter change should be driven by the public interest, not by the needs of incumbents, we should treat that as a red line, not a suggestion.

Second, we need to support the reforms they are still pushing. 

That includes a real anti-dynasty law, not one written with escape hatches; responsible and transparent discussions on economic amendments; clearer and fairer rules on impeachment that preserve accountability; and stronger protections for free expression and media independence. 

If we say we honor them but do nothing when they come to the Senate or the public with specific proposals, then our “tribute” is just noise.

Third, we should document and learn from them while we still can. 

Their interviews, lectures, hearings, and written statements are a living syllabus on how a democracy tries to correct itself. 

Treating their appearances as just viral clips we scroll past is a waste. 

At the very least, my job—as someone who writes and thinks out loud in public—is to listen carefully, connect the dots, and help more people see why these elders still matter.

🟥 THE GENERATIONAL HANDOFF

The truth is, they are already on overtime. 

Every time I see Azcuna walk us through another complicated ruling, or Monsod sit through another Senate hearing, or Carpio explain yet another Charter-change gambit, I feel grateful and a bit ashamed. 

Grateful that they still answer when the Republic calls; ashamed that we keep needing them to rescue us from problems we should have learned to solve by now.

At some point, we cannot keep running to them every time the house is on fire. 

The best way to honor them is not just to praise their courage, but to build a political culture where that kind of courage is no longer rare, and where accountability and decency are enforced by living institutions, not just by a handful of elders standing between the Constitution and those who would gut it.

While they are still here, the least we can do is say thank you while they can still hear it—thank you for writing and defending this Charter, thank you for showing up again when you shouldn’t have had to—and then ask ourselves a harder question: when it is finally our turn, will we be ready to stand where they are standing now?

🟥 42-1: THE HOUSE DECIDES ON MARCOS

[A Backgrounder]

For three days, the House Committee on Justice sat in session. Forty-six lawmakers. Two impeachment complaints. One president's fate on the table.

On the afternoon of February 4, the gavel came down.

42-1 on the first complaint. 39-7 on the second.

Both dismissed for being insufficient in substance. Ferdinand Marcos Jr. survives his first impeachment attempt.

🟥 TWO COMPLAINTS, TWO ENDORSERS

The first complaint arrived on January 19. Lawyer Andre de Jesus filed it. Rep. Jett Nisay of Pusong Pinoy signed on as endorser.

A week later, the second complaint followed. This one came from the left. Former Gabriela Rep. Liza Maza led the filing. ACT Teachers Rep. Antonio Tinio, Gabriela Rep. Sarah Jane Elago, and Kabataan Rep. Renee Louise Co put their names behind it.

Two complaints. Two different camps. The same target.

Both cleared the first hurdle. The committee found them sufficient in form. The signatures were in order. The format was correct. The procedural boxes were checked.

Substance was a different matter.

🟥 WHAT THE COMPLAINTS ALLEGED

The De Jesus complaint came with five grounds:

- Graft and corruption through kickbacks and fictitious projects
- Culpable violation of the Constitution
- Betrayal of public trust for failing to veto unprogrammed appropriations and unconstitutional provisions in the 2023-2026 budgets
- Unfitness to serve due to alleged drug addiction, citing Sen. Imee Marcos' own statement about her brother
- Creating the Independent Commission for Infrastructure to allegedly protect corrupt associates

There was also this: Marcos signed the 2025 budget that allowed the transfer of excess PhilHealth funds to the National Treasury. The Supreme Court later struck this down as unconstitutional. The complaint asked: why did the President sign it in the first place?

The Makabayan complaint focused on three betrayal of public trust allegations:

- Institutionalizing systemic corruption through the BBM Parametric Formula
- Exercising discretionary power over unprogrammed appropriations
- Direct personal involvement in budgetary insertions and kickback schemes

Raymond Palatino, Bayan secretary general, framed it in terms that left no room for ambiguity: "Billions of pesos cannot be stolen without the president's knowledge. Marcos himself signed and implemented the budget. He is the one who initiated the theft and plunder."

🟥 THE BBM PARAMETRIC FORMULA

At the center of both complaints was a budget mechanism with an unfortunate acronym.

BBM. Baselined, Balanced, and Managed.

Former DPWH Undersecretary Maria Catalina Cabral created it in 2022. The formula determines how "allocable funds" get distributed to congressional districts for DPWH projects. The executive branch sets the ceiling. Lawmakers decide which projects eat from that ceiling.

On paper, it looks like decentralized planning.

To critics, it is pork barrel with a new name. The Makabayan complaint went further. They called it an "apparatus for plunder."

🟥 THE ZALDY CO ALLEGATIONS

Both complaints leaned heavily on one man's word.

Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co. Former Ako Bicol representative. Once an ally of the administration. Now a fugitive with an arrest warrant.

In late 2024, Co released a series of videos. The claims were explosive:

- He facilitated P100 billion in budget insertions for the 2025 budget under Marcos' direct instructions
- P56 billion in kickbacks from DPWH flood control projects passed through him to Marcos and House Speaker Martin Romualdez from 2022 to 2025
- He personally delivered P1 billion in cash to a middleman for Marcos in December 2024

Marcos denied everything. He challenged Co to come home and say it under oath.

Co has not returned. He remains abroad, beyond the reach of Philippine courts. His allegations now sit at the heart of two dismissed impeachment complaints.

🟥 DAY ONE: THE WALKOUT AND THE NOTARIZATION ISSUE

February 2. The Batasan Pambansa complex. The Committee on Justice convened.

Before the debate on substance could begin, Rep. Rufus Rodriguez raised a procedural question. The second complaint, he argued, had a notarization problem. Was it properly executed? The committee spent time on this before moving forward.

Not everyone wanted to move forward.

Rep. Bienvenido Abante rose from his seat. He had seen enough.

"Realizing that this is actually an exercise in futility and a waste of the people's money, I therefore would like to walk out of this hearing right now."

He walked out. The cameras followed him briefly. Then they returned to the hearing.

By the end of the day, both complaints were declared sufficient in form. The real fight would come the next day.

🟥 DAY TWO: THE ARGUMENTS BEGIN

February 3. The question moved from form to substance.

Committee Chair Rep. Gerville Luistro set the terms: "For a complaint to be sufficient in substance, it must present facts clearly, demonstrating that the official personally engaged in or willfully ignored actions undermining the integrity of the office."

That was the bar. The debate that followed was whether the complaints cleared it.

Vice Chair Rep. Ysabel Maria Zamora spoke for the majority. Her position was unambiguous.

"The accusations are not factual allegations. A lot of what is written are mere speculations or conjectures arising from hearsay… just to connect the President to the charges."

She was not done.

"To say that the Office of the President has become the command center of a criminal enterprise is not only unfounded, but is also a mere dramatic rhetoric."

Rep. Rufus Rodriguez called the claims "legal conclusions without any foundation."

Rep. Alfredo Garbin reframed the entire complaint: "This pleads a policy critique. It does not plead an impeachable offense nor a willful constitutional breach."

Rep. Janice Degamo offered something closer to a character defense: "When the President put his signature on those documents, perhaps he was thinking that these projects would benefit the public."

Rep. Jan Padiernos addressed the complaint's lone endorser directly: "No, omission is not a basis for impeachment."

The vote came. 42 said insufficient. 1 said sufficient. 3 abstained.

The lone vote for sufficiency belonged to Rep. Jett Nisay. He endorsed the complaint. He stood by it alone.

The three abstentions came from the Makabayan bloc. Their reasoning: this was not their complaint. They would wait for their turn.

🟥 THE MINORITY PUSHES BACK

Not everyone accepted the majority's framing.

Sen. Leila de Lima sat as a resource person. She listened to the speeches defending the President. Then she spoke.

"I cannot help but notice that a couple of previous speakers are, in fact, addressing potential defenses on behalf of the respondent."

The room had turned, she suggested, from evaluation to advocacy.

She pressed further: "If you apply that standard to all complaints and enforce personal knowledge, no one would ever file an impeachment complaint."

And then: "We cannot simply ignore these factual allegations."

Sen. Chel Diokno took a different approach. At this stage, he argued, the committee should assume the allegations are true. The only question should be: if true, do they constitute an impeachable offense?

Rep. Antonio Tinio did not mince words: "It is not their job to advocate for the President. Yet, it appears that is what transpired, as they evaluated the evidence."

🟥 DAY THREE: THE SECOND VOTE

February 4. The Makabayan complaint's turn.

The arguments ran along familiar lines.

Rep. Garbin: "A planning allocation formula is not illegal on its face. Government budgeting necessarily involves policy priorities."

He added: "The phrase 'priorities of leaders,' even if quoted, is not proof of graft."

Zamora returned to her theme: "Having an imperfect policy direction does not equate to an impeachable offense."

Rep. Mauricio Domogan called the corruption allegations "too vague."

Rep. Wesley Gatchalian delivered what became the line of the day: "President cannot be impeached on news articles."

The vote came. 39 said insufficient. 7 said sufficient.

Both complaints were now dead at the committee level.

🟥 WHAT THE COMPLAINANTS SAID

Liza Maza chose her words carefully. The complaint, she said, felt "prematurely murdered."

Then she issued a warning: "The public will demand justice from this process."

Tinio remained defiant: "It's clear, the committee really seems determined to kill the complaints at this stage alone. The grounds for impeachment are sufficient."

They lost the vote. They did not concede the argument.

🟥 WHAT THE PALACE SAID

Malacañang responded the same day.

Press Secretary Cesar Castro said the President was pleased. "He stated that the correct process was adhered to."

Castro added: "We are pleased that the process was followed and that lawmakers saw the truth."

His final word was brief: "Let's move forward."

🟥 WHAT HAPPENS NEXT

The committee report now goes to the plenary for ratification.

Chair Luistro offered a caveat: "To be very clear, the process is not yet finished. Until the plenary decides on the fate of the impeachment against the President, it is not completely over."

But the math is not complicated. The supermajority that controls the House is aligned with the President. The plenary vote will almost certainly mirror what happened in committee.

Marcos remains in office. The first impeachment attempt of his presidency is, for all practical purposes, dead.

Whether it stays buried is another question.

🟥 WHEN DYNASTIES PROMISE TO LIMIT THEMSELVES

Watching the first hearings on the anti-political dynasty bills in the House Committee on Suffrage and Electoral Reforms and hearing that the Senate will take up its own version, something felt off.

The same surnames that have dominated our ballots for decades are now sitting in front of microphones, talking about how to restrain people like themselves. 

While they were thanking each other for a “historic” discussion, my brain went somewhere darker: if I were a corrupt dynastic politician who still wants to steal for another thirty years, how would I adjust to this?

This is me thinking through that out loud.

🟥 THE GEOGRAPHIC SCATTER

First move: don’t shrink the dynasty, spread it out.

The Dy–Marcos-style approach, whether in HB 6771 or the newer House versions, is built around limiting relatives in the same jurisdiction or the same level of government. It sounds strict until you notice the obvious escape route: you just move your relatives around. 

If I’m that corrupt politician, I’m already drawing a map:  

- one kid for Congress,  
- another for mayor in a nearby city,  
- a sibling in the province,  
- someone else down at the barangay level.  

Because the rules look at “same area” and “same level,” a clan can legally occupy multiple layers at once—congressional, provincial, city, municipal, barangay. 

The bill pretends dynasties are stationary; dynasties, in real life, expand horizontally and vertically across positions and territories. 

On paper, it looks like the family retreated. In practice, it just learned to scatter better.

🟥 THE POSITION SWAP

Then there’s the rotation game.

We’ve seen how families treat term limits like musical chairs: a governor trades places with the vice governor, a mayor swaps with a spouse or child, they alternate every three terms and keep going. 

The Constitution and local government laws were supposed to stop people from clinging to one post forever, but nothing stops a family from holding that post in turns. 

If I’m that trapo, this bill doesn’t scare me. I’ll just plan the sequence in advance: three terms for me, three for my wife, three for my son. As long as we obey the letter of term limits, the position never leaves the bloodline. 

What bothers me is how “legal” it will all look. No cheating needed. You just abuse a rule that was too polite to imagine you’d be this shameless.

🟥 THE PARTY-LIST PARKING

Then we get to the party-list system.

Right now, senators are publicly saying they’ll tackle both party-list reforms and the anti-dynasty bill, while in the House, the focus is mostly on local and district posts. 

Critics of HB 6771 and related proposals have been pointing out for a while that party-list is the soft spot: vague coverage, weak enforcement, lots of room for creative interpretation. 

If I’m a dynasty, I see that as parking space. The district seat is blocked? Fine. I’ll park my kid under a “sectoral” group. The surname might not even show up in the party name.  

It will look like “representation.” But it’s just the same family entering through a side door.

I keep thinking about how this system was supposed to give marginalized groups a voice. Instead, you can see how easily it becomes an overflow lot for political families once the main road gets crowded.

🟥 THE APPOINTIVE SHADOW GOVERNMENT

Even if the law becomes strict on elected posts, appointments are another universe.

The bills being discussed, including what the House is now hearing, talk mostly about who can run for office and where. 

They barely graze appointments—who gets to be provincial administrator, who chairs the bids and awards committee, who sits as “consultant” with a generous contract. 

If I’m that corrupt official, this is where I move my relatives and closest allies. My name might be the only one on the ballot, but when you look inside city hall or the capitol, it’s full of family and loyalists handling money, permits, and contracts.

The law can tell my relatives “you can’t run here.” It won’t necessarily tell them “you can’t control things from the back office.”

Dito ako hindi mapalagay: voters only see the candidate on the tarp. They don’t see who signs the purchase orders.

🟥 THE KAMAG-ANAK INC. ECONOMY

“Kamag-anak Inc.” has been around as a phrase since the Cory years, but it still explains so much of how power works. 

If I want to keep stealing safely, I don’t just keep my relatives in government. I keep them around government. 

One runs the construction firm that always wins the DPWH and LGU projects. Another owns the supplier for medical equipment. Someone else controls the security agency or janitorial contractor. 

On paper, these are private businesses playing by the rules of public bidding. In reality, they orbit one political sun.

No anti-dynasty bill can fix that by itself. Now you’re in the territory of procurement law, conflict of interest rules, lifestyle checks, and actual enforcement. And we all know how often officials actually get punished for that. 

So even if they pass a decent anti-dynasty law, Kamag-anak Inc. can keep making money unless we also go after how contracts are awarded.

🟥 THE DUMMY CANDIDATE

If the surname becomes too controversial, there’s always the dummy.

Picture it: I’m a well-known corrupt politician. My family name is toxic in some sectors, and an anti-dynasty law suddenly takes effect. What do I do? 

I find someone I trust—business partner, long-time ally, someone who owes me everything—and I push them to run.

I fund the campaign, lend them the machinery, introduce them to my donors, and, if they win, I stay right beside them as “adviser.”

On paper, I’m gone. The dynasty looks broken. But the money flows in the same direction, the projects go to the same companies, and the same people pull strings behind closed doors.

You can’t legislate utang na loob. You can’t legislate secret deals. That’s what makes this problem so slippery.

🟥 THE DELAY AND DILUTE MOVE

Then there’s the part that’s happening in front of us right now: the careful softening of the law.

The House is proudly calling these hearings “long overdue” and “historic,” with the Speaker reminding everyone that the people have waited nearly 40 years for an enabling law on dynasties. The Senate is talking about how this might finally be the Congress that moves an anti-dynasty bill forward. 

At the same time, the Dy–Marcos HB 6771 has already been criticized as shallow, designed to pass because it doesn’t really hurt dynasties. 

Analysts warn it regulates the easiest part—names on ballots—while ignoring how dynasties actually survive: through spreading across levels, rotating seats, and running networks that go beyond immediate family.

If I’m a dynasty lawmaker, this is perfect. I support the safe version. 

I drag my feet on any stronger proposals that try to ban up to the fourth degree or cover more positions. Then I go home and say, “May ginawa kami. We fulfilled the Constitution.” 

On TV, it looks like progress. Down here, the same families are just better instructed on how to adapt.

🟥 THE DORMANT DYNASTY

There’s also the “pahinga muna” strategy.

Research on dynasties in the Philippines and other democracies shows how some families “go dormant”: they step back from visible office, focus on business, build money and connections, then return when things are calmer or when the law ends up weaker than feared.

If I’m a long-game kind of trapo, I can afford to lie low for one election cycle. Keep one relative somewhere small to maintain presence, pull everyone else out of the spotlight, then come back when people are tired, distracted, or desperate for “experienced” leaders again.

We like to say “naalis na sila.” The truth is, many of them just wait.

🟥 WHY I’M EVEN WRITING THIS

I don’t enjoy thinking this way. It feels grim to sit in front of these hearings and immediately try to imagine how bad actors will twist whatever law comes out. [youtube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yeLv2l9i-Fc)

But if they’re planning their moves, we should be planning ours.

I’m not pretending to be neutral here. I want a real anti-dynasty law—one that bites, one that doesn’t just teach dynasties how to rearrange themselves. 

What I’m afraid of is exactly what critics of HB 6771 warned about: a law that looks good on paper, satisfies the Constitution “in form,” but leaves the machine intact. 

So if this bill, or something like it, passes, here’s what I’m personally watching for in my own city and province:  

- same clans, but more spread out across different levels,  
- obvious seat-swapping between family members,  
- relatives and loyalists quietly filling appointed posts,  
- family-linked businesses still bagging contracts,  
- “new faces” who smell a lot like old patrons.  

If we recognize these as tactics—and not just “ganito talaga ang politika”—we’re harder to fool.

The law might or might not have teeth by the time they’re done amending it. But whether it’s weak or strong, dynasties are already thinking about how to survive it.

We should be thinking about how not to keep voting for that survival.

🟥 THE SEAT, THE JOKE, THE WARNING

[An MCT Commentary] 

Loren Legarda sat in the Senate President’s chair, and Migz Zubiri called her “Madam President” while the minority bloc was nowhere to be found.

Natawa akonwhen the session returned  after an hour of break. Everyone was smiling like someone just said an internal joke but  at the same time you know someone’s (in this case, the minority bloc) being put in their place.

Because after the coup talk, that chair became a message: the majority is still the majority, and the numbers are still holding.

🟥 WHY THE MAJORITY LOOKS “STABLE” RIGHT NOW

I’m going to say this plainly: the perception now is that the majority, for all its flaws, is the stable choice for the country.

Sotto himself said the majority thwarted a coup attempt, and the coverage around that moment emphasized the absence of Cayetano’s minority bloc and the majority’s show of unity.

And yes, people read that unity through the lens of what’s coming—Sara Duterte’s impeachment fight is the elephant in the room, and a pro-Duterte minority taking control would be a gift to her.

So when the majority “holds the fort,” it doesn’t just protect Sotto’s chair. It protects the country from a Senate that looks like it’s being rearranged for self-interest.

One-sentence truth: I may not like some heads in this majority, but I’m still rooting for it to stay intact. So long as I see that it benefits the nation. 

🟥 WHY LEGARDA IN THAT SEAT FEELS LIKE A MIDDLE FINGER AIMED AT THE MINORITY

The rumored plot was basically: lure Legarda, flip her, get the magic 13, install new leadership.

Legarda refused, according to Sotto.

Then she gets to preside—even briefly—and Zubiri calls her “Madam President” like a teasing prophecy.

That’s the majority openly telling the Tsinators: “You have no power here.”

It’s also them saying: “You don’t get to use her as your battering ram.”

🟥 THE OTHER PERCEPTION: A LOVING MOTHER WITH POWER

Now the part that makes me stop laughing.

If Legarda ever becomes Senate President—even via term-sharing talk, even if everyone insists it’s “katuwaan”—there’s a perception that immediately attaches to her: that she’ll use the post to shield her son, or at least soften the blows against him.

And I get why people think that, because the “she lobbied for him” story isn’t pulled out of thin air. The perception is there: she didn’t just stand aside; she moved for him.

So when I ask myself, what will a loving mother do for her son if she has the Senate presidency—honestly? Most mothers would move heaven and earth.

That instinct is human.

In government, that instinct becomes a governance problem. A conflict of interest. 

And that’s the scary part: even if she does nothing improper, the perception alone can corrode trust—especially when Leandro’s energy firms are already under heavy public scrutiny.

🟥 WHY I’M STILL ON THE SIDE OF “HOLD THE FORT”

I’m not praising the majority as saints. I’m saying, given the current alignment, a Senate that resists a pro-Duterte takeover looks like the more responsible option right now.

If the minority is seen as self-interest-first—and if its power play is seen as designed to shape what happens next, including the Sara Duterte impeachment fight—then keeping them out of the driver’s seat is, to me, the safer outcome for the Philippines.

The minority, in this reading, is a threat to the country and a blessing to Sara Duterte.

The majority, with all its imperfections, is the one holding the line.

But here’s my condition, and it’s not negotiable.

If Legarda’s name keeps floating around that chair, she needs to meet the public halfway: address the Leandro questions with transparency, not with silence, not with “joke lang,” not with vibes.

Because stability that rests on unspoken deals is still instability.

And we’ve had enough of that.
